text: The surgeon performs lateral dissection during a sigmoid resection for diverticulitis, focusing on separating tissue layers on the lateral side of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection approach, initiating the procedure by carefully separating tissue layers in the sigmoid colon area.
The surgeon skeletonizes the superior hemorrhoidal artery, carefully dissecting and exposing the vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon creates a small window around the superior hemorrhoidal artery and proceeds to dissect it.
The surgeon performs additional medial dissection, continuing the approach from the medial side towards the lateral aspect of the surgical field.
The surgeon observes that the retroperitoneum appears more adherent than usual, indicating increased tissue stickiness during the dissection.
The surgeon transitions from medial to lateral dissection, adjusting the approach to continue the procedure.
The surgeon marks the colon at the proximal margin, indicating the presumed location for resection.
The surgeon identifies the retroperitoneum along the white line to the colon, noting its vascular nature.
The surgeon encounters difficulty as the tissue between the colon and the retroperitoneum does not separate easily, indicating a challenging dissection.
The video shows a nearly unedited lateral mobilization of the colon, highlighting the surgical technique used to separate the colon from the lateral attachments.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and create a window between Girota's fascia and the mesentery, progressing towards the spleen. This technique is chosen for efficiency and to manage the vascularity in the area.
The surgeon completes the division of the mesentery and proceeds to take additional length for the procedure.
The surgeon encounters omental adhesions to the left anterior abdominal wall near the splenic flexure, which are visible during the procedure.
The surgeon lowers the omental adhesions to fully expose the splenic flexure, preparing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon demonstrates access to the splenic flexure area for dissection using a robotic system, without altering the patient's position or redocking the robot.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon repositions the instruments to obtain a clearer view of the surgical field near the splenic flexure, addressing omental adhesions to the left anterior abdominal wall.
The spleen is visible in the surgical field, indicating proximity to the splenic flexure during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the mobilization of the colon, focusing on the lateral aspect as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery from the kidney, allowing the colon to swing medially away from the sidewall, improving exposure at the splenic flexure area.
The surgeon adjusts the view by repositioning the instruments, starting from a lower position and working upwards towards the splenic flexure for better visualization.
The surgeon performs dissection down towards the area affected by diverticular disease, manipulating tissue to improve access and visibility.
The surgeon approaches a sticky area during the dissection, likely involving adhesions or dense tissue, requiring careful manipulation to continue mobilizing the colon.
The colon is positioned over the left myopectenial orifice, obscuring the internal ring.
The surgeon brings down the colon, revealing an incarcerated hernia containing portions of the colon and epiploic fat.
The surgeon returns to the medial side to attempt connecting the previously dissected lateral and medial spaces, focusing on the area near the diverticular disease.
The surgical field shows dense scarring between the left dome of the bladder and the left myopectineal orifice.
The surgeon identifies a hernia in the surgical field, visible against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon detaches the remaining tissue attachments from the left pelvic sidewall, completing the dissection in this area.
As the surgeon progresses lower, adhesions to the rectum become visible, requiring careful dissection to separate these attachments.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes tissue from the deep pelvis to improve visibility, focusing on the remaining attachments.
The surgeon identifies the area where the sigmoid colon is visibly fused to the rectum, highlighting the anatomical fusion between these structures.
The surgeon navigates the left-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, revealing a fold of fat without any bowel or lumen visible.
The surgeon unfolds the tissue in the deep pelvis to determine the distal margin, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the colectomy.
The surgeon proceeds to take the proximal margin of the colon, ensuring the tissue is adequately separated and prepared for resection.
The surgeon continues dissection straight up to the bowel wall, approaching the area of the previously selected margin.
The surgeon cleans the colon at the distal margin, ensuring the area is free of debris and ready for further surgical steps.
The surgeon checks the indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence to assess perfusion as they prepare to divide the colon proximally.
The surgeon divides the distal section of the colon at the previously cleaned margin, completing the separation of the bowel segment.
The surgeon prepares to perform an intracorporeal anastomosis, aligning the bowel ends for connection following the division of the colon.
The surgeon has sewn the two staple lines together using two OPDS devices and is now performing a proctotomy, creating an incision in the rectum.
The surgeon performs a colotomy, creating an incision in the colon as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins sewing the inner layer of the anastomosis, moving towards the patient's left side.
The surgeon takes large, full-thickness bites of tissue during the anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 locking suture to secure the tissue.
The surgeon begins placing the inner suture, sewing towards the patient's right side, using the same technique as before.
The surgeon places the stitches precisely in the desired locations on the inner layer of the surgical site.
The surgeon ties two stitches together over the top, securing the suture line.
The surgeon sews another layer using 2-0 PDS suture to imbricate the inner layer, adding reinforcement to the previously placed sutures.
The surgeon ties the current suture to one of the originally placed threosilk stay sutures, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis, finalizing the connection between the two sections of the colon.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the completed anastomosis, checking for any visible signs of leakage at the surgical site.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal opening of the hernia, using sutures to secure the tissue layers.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the completed anastomosis, with the test results showing no leakage.
The surgical specimen is extracted through a fan retractor and steel instrument.
